# Chapter 16

[p4z7n1x3] They left before dawn because the heat was a cop and the daylight was a witness. The compound folded itself up in the dark—tarps dragged off hoods, tools shoved into crates, solar batteries strapped down like captives. Flynn carried a jerrycan that still smelled of old gasoline and felt the weight of it in his wrists like guilt. Cage moved through it all loose and humming, as if a move was just another kind of lap.

[k9d2v8c5] Wrench had been up all night, sitting on the tailgate of the flatbed with his boots in the dust, watching the horizon where the boneyard lights used to be. He looked older than he was, skin drawn tight over the bones of his face, eyes like two dim coals. When Diesel handed him keys he didn’t take them right away. He just stared at the metal like it was a verdict. “You sure,” he said. It wasn’t a question.

[u1h6t0r9] Diesel didn’t waste words. She pointed to the Chevelle, the one they’d been babying, the one that ran clean and mean when it wanted. “You take her,” she said. “Easy on the throttle till she warms.” Her voice stayed flat but Flynn saw her hand hover a second too long near Wrench’s shoulder, like she wanted to steady him and refused the weakness. Wrench nodded. The nod looked like surrender.

[s3m8a2j7] Birdie climbed into the Charger with Cage like it was church. He was grinning in the dim light, teeth bright, hands twitching with a hunger he couldn’t hide. Cage slid behind the wheel and for a second the cabin lit up blue from the dash screen and Flynn saw Cage’s face in that light—calm, almost tender, as if he loved the machine more than he’d ever loved a person. Then the screen dimmed and the face went back to shadow.

[b5q1w9e4] Flynn rode with Diesel in the old service truck because there weren’t enough seats and because Diesel didn’t want him near Cage’s laughter. The truck smelled of coolant and sweat and the stale bite of cigarettes. Diesel kept both hands on the wheel, knuckles pale, eyes never resting. “If he starts,” she said, not looking at Flynn, “you keep your mouth shut. You hear me.” Flynn said he heard. He wasn’t sure it mattered.

[n7f3y6l2] They rolled out in a staggered line, lights off, guided by moon and memory and the pale smear of roadway. The desert around them was black and wide and indifferent. In the rearview Flynn saw the Charger’s silhouette closing and opening as Cage played with distance, like a predator testing how far the herd would run. The engines sounded wrong in the quiet world, too alive, too human.

[t0c9g4p8] The plan was simple. Cross the old county highway, cut south along a service road that ran beside the solar farms, then drop into an abandoned subdivision where the streets still existed under sand. From there they’d disappear into the wash country and come up near the border days later. Diesel had said it like an itinerary. Cage had said nothing, which meant he agreed or didn’t care.

[r2v5k1d7] Wrench drove in front because he knew the roads from when they were still used. The Chevelle’s taillights were taped over, just two slits of red like eyes half open. Flynn watched that car drift slightly in its lane, not from wind but from the man behind the wheel. Every time Wrench corrected, the rear end wiggled, the suspension talking back. Diesel muttered, “Jesus,” and kept driving.

[x8s0h3m6] Cage eased the Charger up behind Wrench and held it there. No headlights, just the low thunder of V8 breathing through cut pipes. Flynn couldn’t see Cage’s eyes but he could feel them, the way a person feels a gun pointed even if they don’t look. Birdie’s head was turned toward the Chevelle like he was watching a show. The darkness between cars tightened.

[e4b7n2q9] Diesel glanced at Flynn. “He’s doing it,” she said. Her voice was smaller than Flynn had heard it. Flynn said, “Doing what.” Diesel shook her head like he was slow on purpose. “Making him run,” she said. “He does it when he’s bored. He does it when someone thinks they can leave.” Flynn’s throat went dry. He watched the Chevelle’s little red slits tremble.

[l1t6p3z8] The county highway opened up—straight, cracked, with sand drifted across it in long pale tongues. The speed climbed without anyone saying so. The engines rose in pitch. The truck shuddered as Diesel pushed it harder than she liked. Flynn saw the Charger pull even closer to the Chevelle until the two shapes were almost one long animal. There was no room for error at that distance. That was the point.

[g9r2s5u0] Wrench looked back once. Flynn couldn’t see his face but he saw the movement, the slight sway of the car as Wrench turned his head and lost a fraction of focus. Cage didn’t look away. The Charger stayed planted behind him, steady as a thought. Then Cage tapped the throttle and the Charger surged, bumper nearly kissing the Chevelle’s rear valance. Flynn felt his stomach drop.

[w3x7c1f4] Diesel whispered, “Don’t.” She didn’t say who she was talking to. Flynn’s implant buzzed with a hydration reminder, a little icon floating in the corner of his vision like a mocking hand offering help. He blinked it away. The world ahead was just road and darkness and two cars about to become one crash. The truck’s headlights were off but the sky had enough moon to show the pale edge of asphalt.

[a2l8e0v5] Cage nudged Wrench. Not a hit, not yet—just a touch, the kind of contact you’d make in a crowded room to remind someone you were behind them. The Chevelle’s rear end twitched. Wrench corrected too hard and the car drifted toward the right shoulder where sand waited like soft teeth. Cage stayed in the lane, patient, watching the wobble travel through the Chevelle’s body.

[c6u1g9r3] Flynn heard Diesel breathe in sharp through her nose. “He’s going to pit him,” she said. The words didn’t sound real. Flynn imagined the physics the way Diesel had taught him: weight transfer, traction, the moment a tire loses grip and doesn’t get it back. At a hundred miles an hour there wasn’t time to learn. There was only time to die and maybe be dragged back by a machine.

[v5n2b7q1] The road dipped and for an instant the cars went light, suspension unloading, tires barely kissing the broken pavement. Cage chose that moment. The Charger slid left and then right in a smooth arc, the front bumper angling toward the Chevelle’s rear quarter panel. It wasn’t reckless. It was practiced. The impact came with a dull sound that Flynn felt in his teeth even from behind.

[h0p8k4d2] The Chevelle snapped sideways. The rear end stepped out to the left, then overcorrected, swinging back hard. Wrench fought it—Flynn could see the brake lights flare and then go dark, the steering inputs translating into sharp jerks of the body. The tires found sand at the shoulder and the car’s right side lifted slightly as if it wanted to climb out of the world. Then it bit.

[j7s3m6x9] The right wheels dug into the drifted sand and the Chevelle tripped like a runner catching a toe. The car rotated, ninety degrees, then more, the whole mass turning into a spinning slab of steel. The driver’s side faced the oncoming darkness. The headlights, taped down, were useless, and the only light was moon and the faint glow of the dash inside the spinning cabin. Flynn saw a flash of Wrench’s hands on the wheel, white-knuckled, and then the car left the road entirely.

[y1c5t0l8] The Chevelle hit the shallow ditch hard enough to bottom out, then launched. For a breath it was airborne, weightless, absurd, a muscle car flying like a bad dream. Then it came down nose-first into the far bank and the sound turned into a long tearing grind. Metal screamed. Glass burst. The car began to roll, end over end, each rotation slower than the last, like the world was forcing it to pay attention.

[d2v9a4w7] Diesel stood on the brakes and the truck’s rear end fishtailed. Flynn’s shoulder slammed into the door. The truck corrected and skidded to a stop on the cracked asphalt. Dust rolled past the windshield in a thick wave, blotting out the ditch for a moment. Flynn tasted grit. His ears rang. In the settling dust he saw the Chevelle upside down, wheels still spinning, body crumpled like it had been folded by a giant hand.

[q8h1r6e3] Cage didn’t stop right away. The Charger idled past the wreck at a slow crawl, as if Cage wanted to see it from every angle. Birdie leaned out the window, shouting something Flynn couldn’t hear. The Charger’s taillights glowed red and steady and then the car circled back and stopped a hundred feet ahead, blocking the lane like a gate. Cage got out slow. He didn’t run.

[f4z7j2s5] Flynn was out of the truck before Diesel could grab him. His boots hit the road and he sprinted into the ditch, sliding in sand, hands out. The smell was hot oil and gasoline and the sharp chemical tang of deployed airbags. The Chevelle’s roof had caved. The windshield was gone. Inside, Wrench hung upside down in the harness, head tilted at an angle that made Flynn’s stomach lurch. Blood ran in slow drops onto the headliner.

[m3k0p9c6] Diesel reached the wreck and dropped to her knees without ceremony. She shoved her arm into the broken cabin, fingers searching for the implant port at the base of Wrench’s skull. Her other hand held a small injector kit, the emergency revival pack she’d kept taped under the truck seat like a talisman. “Wrench,” she said once. Not loud. Not pleading. Just a name, like a command.

[u7n1x4b8] Wrench’s eyes were open but they weren’t seeing. His mouth moved and no sound came. The implant at his neck blinked a weak green, then amber, warning lights like a dying phone. Diesel cursed under her breath and slapped the injector into the port with practiced violence. The pack hissed. Somewhere inside Wrench’s body a pump kicked, a machine deciding the meat was still worth saving.

[s6m2a9j0] Cage stood above them on the edge of the ditch, hands in his pockets, watching like he watched engines. His face was unreadable in the moonlight, but Flynn saw something like satisfaction in the set of his jaw. Birdie hovered behind him, suddenly quiet. Cage said, “He gonna make it?” Diesel didn’t answer. She kept her fingers pressed to Wrench’s throat, feeling for a pulse like faith.

[b1q5w8e2] Wrench convulsed. His body jerked against the harness and his head smacked the crushed roof with a dull knock. A breath came in, wet and ragged, and then another. The implant beeped and in Flynn’s vision an ad tried to open—some smiling face offering a pain-free recovery plan with subscription tiers. Flynn flinched like he’d been hit. Diesel reached up and smashed the interface dark with the heel of her hand.

[n9f3y0l7] Wrench made a sound then, a low animal noise that wasn’t language. His eyes found Flynn’s for half a second and Flynn saw terror there, pure and childlike, a man waking into the knowledge of his own brokenness. Wrench tried to lift a hand and couldn’t. The harness held him upside down like a butcher’s hook. Flynn grabbed his wrist anyway, as if touch could keep him from falling apart.

[t4c8g1p6] Diesel cut the belt with a knife and Wrench dropped into the sand in a heavy collapse. His legs didn’t fold right. One knee bent backward in a way knees aren’t meant to. Flynn gagged and swallowed it down. Diesel checked his pupils, his breathing, the implant status, all the little signs that the system was doing what it promised. The system could restart a heart. It couldn’t remake a spine.

[r7v2k5d9] Cage crouched beside Wrench and for a moment Flynn thought he was going to help. Cage reached out and wiped a smear of blood from Wrench’s cheek with his thumb, gentle as a lover. Wrench flinched and Cage smiled. “You always were a little loose in the corners,” Cage said. “Gotta trust the throttle.” He sounded like he was giving a lesson. Flynn felt something inside him go cold.

[x0s6h3m8] Diesel stood up fast. “Get away from him,” she said. Her voice finally had heat in it. Cage looked up at her, eyes bright, amused. “You mad at me, Senna?” he asked, using the real name like a blade. Diesel’s hands clenched. Flynn saw the moment she imagined killing him and the moment she remembered she couldn’t. Cage stood and stretched, joints cracking, as if the crash had been a workout.

[e2b9n4q7] They dragged Wrench to the truck on a tarp, the way you drag a dead deer. Wrench whimpered the whole time, a soft sound that didn’t stop. Each bump made his body jolt wrong. Flynn kept looking back at the Chevelle, upside down in the ditch, steam rising from the engine bay like breath leaving. Cage walked behind them, whistling again, and Birdie trailed with his hands at his sides like he’d forgotten what to do with them.

[l5t1p8z3] When they got Wrench into the truck’s back seat, Diesel wedged him with blankets to keep his neck still. Wrench’s eyes rolled, unfocused, and his lips moved around words that never made it out. Flynn climbed in beside him and held the man’s hand, feeling the tremor under the skin, the way the body tried to live despite everything. Outside the windshield Cage climbed back into the Charger and revved the engine once, a sharp bark that echoed off empty land. Then he pulled out onto the highway and led them south, leaving the wreck behind like a marker, like a warning, like a joke the desert would keep telling after they were gone.

[p1d7k3m9] The truck chased the Charger’s taillights through the thinning dark. Wrench’s breathing came in little broken packets. Every few minutes his implant tried to speak—soft chimes, a soothing voice that wanted permission to administer analgesics and stream calming content. Diesel kept swiping it away without looking. Flynn watched Wrench’s jaw clench, the pain too big to name, and he understood something ugly: you could be kept alive by law and firmware and still be wrecked beyond any rescue.

[z6v2r8c0] At the first pale hint of day they passed the solar fields, black panels stretching to the horizon like a dead lake. Between the rows small maintenance drones moved, indifferent, their lights blinking. Flynn imagined one of them lifting into the air, catching the convoy on some algorithmic glance, marking them for later. Cage didn’t slow. He wanted the world to look. He wanted it to know there were still people who could break the rules and keep moving.

[h3n9s1q5] Diesel drove with her mouth set hard. Flynn could feel words in her, a pressure building, but she kept it behind her teeth. In the side mirror Flynn saw Birdie’s face in the Charger’s passenger window, pale and stunned, his earlier grin gone. Cage’s profile was steady, a man in his element. Flynn held Wrench’s hand until his own fingers went numb and stared at the road unspooling south, thinking of the ditch behind them and the ditch ahead, and how Cage seemed to live only in the moment between.
